title: Developmental Disabilities — Communication, Behavior Techniques & Dual Diagnosis (Set 3)
subtitle: AAC, functional behavior assessment, teaching methods & mental illness in DD
scenario: Supporting communication, using behavior-support and teaching techniques, and recognizing co-occurring mental illness in people with developmental disabilities.

steps.q: A nonverbal client uses a picture board and a speech-generating device to communicate. These are examples of:

opts.rat: Picture boards and speech-generating devices are augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) tools that support people with limited or no speech.
These are communication supports, not restraints.
AAC supports communication; it is not punitive.
AAC has nothing to do with medication.

steps.q: A visual (picture) schedule benefits many clients with developmental disabilities by:

opts.text: Confusing them with too much information
opts.rat: Visual schedules are designed to clarify, not confuse.

opts.text: Helping them anticipate the day and reducing anxiety around transitions
opts.rat: Visual schedules make routines predictable, support understanding, and reduce anxiety around transitions.

opts.text: Replacing the need for any staff support
opts.rat: They support—rather than replace—staff interaction and care.
A schedule is a support, not a punishment.

steps.q: A functional behavior assessment is used to determine:

opts.rat: This is irrelevant to behavior assessment.

opts.text: How to punish the behavior fastest
opts.rat: The goal is understanding function to guide support, not punishment.

opts.text: The purpose (function) a behavior serves for the client
opts.rat: A functional behavior assessment identifies why a behavior occurs—its function, such as gaining attention, escaping a demand, sensory input, or obtaining a tangible item.
This has nothing to do with behavior function.

steps.q: Common FUNCTIONS of a challenging behavior include gaining attention, obtaining an item, sensory input, and:

opts.rat: This is not a behavioral function.
This is unrelated to behavior.
This is not a function of behavior.

opts.text: Escaping or avoiding a demand or situation
opts.rat: Behaviors often function to escape/avoid a task or situation, alongside attention, tangible, and sensory functions.

steps.q: When staff record the Antecedent, Behavior, and Consequence (ABC) of an event, the ANTECEDENT is:

opts.text: What happened right before the behavior
opts.rat: The antecedent is what occurred immediately before the behavior; ABC data helps identify triggers and patterns.
That is the consequence, not the antecedent.
The antecedent precedes the behavior.
The antecedent is the immediate preceding event, not a diagnosis.

steps.q: Teaching a new skill by reinforcing small steps that get closer and closer to the goal behavior is called:

opts.rat: Seclusion is a restrictive intervention, not a teaching method.
Shaping reinforces successive approximations—progressively closer attempts—until the target skill is achieved.
Extinction is withholding reinforcement, not building a skill in steps.
Restraint is not a teaching technique.

steps.q: Gradually reducing the cues given to a client as they master a skill is known as:

opts.rat: The goal is to reduce prompts, promoting independence, not increase them.
This is unrelated to fading.
Fading gradually removes prompts/cues as the client becomes able to perform the skill independently.
Fading is a teaching support, not punishment.

steps.q: In a token economy, a client:

opts.rat: A token economy rewards desired behavior; it is not a punishment system for existing.
Tokens are earned for and exchanged toward reinforcers, not seclusion.
Tokens are not linked to medication.

opts.text: Earns tokens for desired behaviors that can be exchanged for reinforcers
opts.rat: A token economy reinforces desired behaviors with tokens the client later exchanges for preferred items or activities.

steps.q: When a previously reinforced behavior is placed on extinction (reinforcement withheld), the technician should expect:

opts.text: A temporary increase in the behavior (extinction burst) before it decreases
opts.rat: An extinction burst—a temporary increase in the behavior—commonly occurs before the behavior decreases; consistency is essential.

opts.text: The behavior to vanish instantly with no change
opts.rat: Behaviors on extinction often briefly increase first, rather than vanishing instantly.

opts.text: The client to develop a new physical illness
opts.rat: Extinction is a behavioral process, not a cause of illness.

opts.text: Immediate permanent improvement in all areas
opts.rat: Extinction affects the targeted behavior, not all behaviors instantly.

steps.q: For a behavior-support plan to be effective, all staff across shifts and settings should:

opts.text: Each use their own personal approach
opts.rat: Inconsistent approaches confuse the client and weaken the plan.

opts.text: Apply the plan consistently the same way
opts.rat: Consistency across all staff and settings is essential; inconsistent responses undermine behavior-support plans.
Skipping the plan breaks the consistency it depends on.

opts.text: Change the plan daily without documentation
opts.rat: Undocumented, constant changes make the plan ineffective.

steps.q: 'Generalization' of a newly learned skill means the client can:

opts.text: Only perform it in the exact training room
opts.rat: That is the opposite of generalization.
Generalization involves retaining and transferring the skill.

opts.text: Use the skill in different settings and with different people
opts.rat: Generalization is applying a learned skill across various settings, people, and situations, not just where it was taught.

opts.text: Perform it only for one specific staff member
opts.rat: Generalization means the skill transfers across people, not just one.

steps.q: Accurate data collection on a client's behavior is important because it:

opts.text: Is only busywork with no purpose
opts.rat: Data directly informs effective care decisions.
Data must be objective and accurate, not guessed.

opts.text: Replaces the need to observe the client
opts.rat: Data comes from careful observation; it does not replace it.

opts.text: Guides and evaluates the behavior-support plan objectively
opts.rat: Objective data reveals patterns, guides the plan, and shows whether interventions are working.

steps.q: A 'dual diagnosis' in this population refers to a person who has:

opts.text: Both a developmental disability and a co-occurring mental illness
opts.rat: Dual diagnosis here means a developmental disability plus a co-occurring mental illness; both are real and treatable.
This is not a clinical concept.

opts.text: A developmental disability that cannot coexist with mental illness
opts.rat: Mental illness can and does co-occur with developmental disability.
Dual diagnosis means two coexisting conditions.

steps.q: A client with a developmental disability shows a marked change from their usual behavior—new withdrawal and loss of interest. The technician should:

opts.text: Assume it is simply part of the disability and ignore it
opts.rat: Attributing new symptoms solely to the disability (diagnostic overshadowing) can miss treatable illness.

opts.text: Report the change, considering a possible mental or physical illness
opts.rat: A change from baseline may signal a treatable mental illness or medical problem; it should be assessed and reported rather than assumed to be 'just the disability.'
This is punitive and ignores a possible illness.

opts.text: Tell the client to cheer up and move on
opts.rat: This dismisses a potentially serious change.

steps.q: When a psychotropic medication is prescribed for a client with a developmental disability, the technician should:

opts.rat: Side effects can and do occur and must be monitored.

opts.text: Give extra doses to keep the client quiet
opts.rat: Extra dosing for convenience is chemical restraint and is prohibited.

opts.text: Monitor for both therapeutic effects and side effects and report changes
opts.rat: Psychotropics in this population are used carefully at the lowest effective dose; monitoring for benefit and adverse effects and reporting changes is essential.

opts.text: Stop reporting once the client is calm
opts.rat: Ongoing monitoring and reporting are always required.

steps.q: Using a sedating medication mainly to make a client with a developmental disability easier to manage, rather than to treat a condition, is:

opts.rat: This is not acceptable practice.
It is never a required or appropriate routine.
Sedating for convenience is highly restrictive, not least restrictive.

opts.text: Chemical restraint, which is not acceptable
opts.rat: Medicating for staff convenience rather than a legitimate treatment purpose is chemical restraint and is prohibited and tightly regulated.

steps.q: To prevent a behavioral crisis, the technician should:

opts.text: Recognize early signs of escalation and intervene calmly and early
opts.rat: Noticing early warning signs and intervening early with calm, supportive strategies helps prevent a full behavioral crisis.

opts.text: Wait until the behavior is severe before acting
opts.rat: Waiting for severe behavior misses the chance to prevent escalation.
Ignoring warning signs allows crises to develop.

opts.text: Immediately use restraint at the first sign
opts.rat: Restraint is a last resort, not a first response to early signs.

steps.q: When choosing an intervention for challenging behavior, the technician should first use:

opts.rat: Starting with the most restrictive option violates least-restrictive principles.
Behavior interventions follow the least-restrictive principle—positive, supportive strategies first, with restrictive measures only as a last resort for safety.
Seclusion is a last resort, not a first-line intervention.
Convenience does not justify restrictive interventions.

steps.q: Which reflects respectful, person-first language?

opts.rat: This is a slur and is disrespectful and unacceptable.
Defining a person by their disability is not person-first or respectful.
Person-first language ('a person with an intellectual disability') puts the individual before the condition and conveys respect.
This is dehumanizing and disrespectful.

steps.q: Teaching a client with a developmental disability functional life skills (such as dressing or making a simple meal) primarily promotes:

opts.rat: The goal is independence, not dependence.
Skill-building is supportive, not punitive.
Life skills support community participation, not isolation.

opts.text: Greater independence and quality of life
opts.rat: Functional life-skills training increases independence, self-esteem, and quality of life.
